title: Quiz: Quantum Amplitude Estimation
description: Test your understanding of quantum amplitude estimation algorithms and their role in quantum speedup

questions.question: What does the angle θ represent in quantum amplitude estimation?

options.text: The phase of the quantum state
The angle such that sin²(θ) equals the target amplitude a
The rotation angle of the QPE circuit

questions.explanation: In QAE, the state is written as |ψ⟩ = sin(θ)|good⟩ + cos(θ)|bad⟩, where sin²(θ) = a is the target amplitude (probability of the good state). The Grover operator rotates by 2θ per application.

questions.question: How does the Grover operator affect the quantum state in amplitude estimation?

options.text: It rotates the state by angle 2θ toward the good state
It performs the quantum Fourier transform

questions.explanation: The Grover operator Q rotates the state by 2θ in the 2D subspace spanned by |good⟩ and |bad⟩. After k applications, the state angle becomes (2k+1)θ, amplifying the good state amplitude.

questions.question: In canonical QAE, what is the role of quantum phase estimation (QPE)?

options.text: To extract the rotation angle θ from the Grover operator's eigenvalue

questions.explanation: QPE is used to measure the eigenvalue of the Grover operator, which is e^(±2iθ). This phase contains the angle θ, from which we can compute a = sin²(θ). This is the key to extracting the amplitude precisely.

questions.question: What happens if you apply too many Grover iterations in amplitude estimation?

options.text: The probability of the good state decreases (overshooting)

questions.explanation: The Grover operator rotates past the optimal angle (90°), causing the good state probability to decrease. The optimal number of iterations is approximately (π/4θ) - 1/2, after which additional iterations reduce accuracy.

questions.question: Iterative QAE requires quantum phase estimation to achieve its speedup.
questions.explanation: False. Iterative QAE is specifically designed to avoid QPE. It uses multiple rounds of Grover iterations followed by measurements, with classical post-processing to narrow down the amplitude estimate. This makes it more suitable for near-term quantum devices.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of Maximum Likelihood QAE (ML-QAE)?

options.text: It can extract information from noisy measurement data
It doesn't require any classical computation

questions.explanation: ML-QAE uses classical maximum likelihood estimation to combine information from measurements at different Grover iteration counts. This makes it robust to noise and can extract more information from imperfect data.

questions.question: How many Grover iterations are needed to achieve accuracy ε in quantum amplitude estimation?
questions.explanation: QAE requires O(1/ε) Grover iterations to achieve accuracy ε. This is the source of the quadratic speedup over classical Monte Carlo, which needs O(1/ε²) samples.

questions.question: What are challenges for implementing QAE on near-term quantum devices? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Deep circuits required for high accuracy
The algorithm is not yet theoretically proven

questions.explanation: Near-term challenges include: (a) deep circuits for controlled Grover iterations, (b) QPE requiring log(1/ε) ancilla qubits, and (c) noise accumulation. The algorithm is theoretically well-established (d is false).
